lineage: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
Demolition of the Idolatrous Temples at Alexandria, and the Consequent Conflict between the Pagans and Christians.

ccel.title: NPNF2-02. Socrates and Sozomenus Ecclesiastical Histories

source.title: NPNF2-02. Socrates and Sozomenus Ecclesiastical Histories

title: Demolition of the Idolatrous Temples at Alexandria, and the Consequent Conflict between the Pagans and Christians.

source.title: NPNF2-02. Socrates and Sozomenus Ecclesiastical Histories

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: At the solicitation of Theophilus bishop of Alexandria the emperor issued an order at this time for the demolition of the heathen temples in that city; commanding also that it should be put in execution under the direction of Theophilus.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Seizing this opportunity, Theophilus exerted himself to the utmost to expose the pagan mysteries to contempt.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: And to begin with, he caused the Mithreum to be cleaned out, and exhibited to public view the tokens of its bloody mysteries.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Then he destroyed the Serapeum, and the bloody rights of the Mithreum he publicly caricatured; the Serapeum also he showed full of extravagant superstitions, and he had the phalli of Priapus carried through the midst of the forum.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: The pagans of Alexandria, and especially the professors of philosophy, were unable to repress their rage at this exposure, and exceeded in revengeful ferocity their outrages on a former occasion: for with one accord, at a preconcerted signal, they rushed impetuously upon the Christians, and murdered every one they could lay hands on.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: The Christians also made an attempt to resist the assailants, and so the mischief was the more augmented.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: This desperate affray was prolonged until satiety of bloodshed put an end to it.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Then it was discovered that very few of the heathens had been killed, but a great number of Christians; while the number of wounded on each side was almost innumerable.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Fear then possessed the pagans on account of what was done, as they considered the emperor’s displeasure.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: For having done what seemed good in their own eyes, and by their bloodshed having quenched their courage, some fled in one direction, some in another, and many quitting Alexandria, dispersed themselves in various cities.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Among these were the two grammarians Helladius and Ammonius, whose pupil I was in my youth at Constantinople.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Helladius was said to be the priest of Jupiter, and Ammonius of Simius.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Thus this disturbance having been terminated, the governor of Alexandria, and the commander-in-chief of the troops in Egypt, assisted Theophilus in demolishing the heathen temples.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: These were therefore razed to the ground, and the images of their gods molten into pots and other convenient utensils for the use of the Alexandrian church; for the emperor had instructed Theophilus to distribute them for the relief of the poor.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: All the images were accordingly broken to pieces, except one statue of the god before mentioned, which Theophilus preserved and set up in a public place; ‘Lest,’ said he, ‘at a future time the heathens should deny that they had ever worshiped such gods.’

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: This action gave great umbrage to Ammonius the grammarian in particular, who to my knowledge was accustomed to say that ‘the religion of the Gentiles was grossly abused in that that single statue was not also molten, but preserved, in order to render that religion ridiculous.’

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Helladius however boasted in the presence of some that he had slain in that desperate onset nine men with his own hand.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Such were the doings at Alexandria at that time.
